summary:
Fresh retinal hyperemic changes are characteristic of chronic hypertension and signify stable vasculopathy rather than acute preeclamptic progression; true signs of superimposed preeclampsia include new-onset proteinuria, thrombocytopenia, or abrupt elevation in systolic/diastolic blood pressure beyond threshold levels.  

$\boxed{\text{D}}$ The correct answer indicating an absence of superimposed preeclampsia is $\boxed{\text{D}}$. 

The summary should be only two sentences long:

Only new-onset symptoms—such as proteinuria, thrombocytopenia, or significantly elevated blood pressure—are diagnostic of superimposed preeclampsia in women with chronic hypertension. Retinal hyperemia represents chronic vascular adaptation and thus does not indicate acute disease exacerbation.  

$\boxed{D}$

Okay, let me now finalize this per all requirements.

First, ensure it’s EXACTLY TWO SENTENCES focusing solely on KEY CLINICAL POINTS related to distinguishing superimposed preeclampsia versus chronic conditions.

Second, end with just \boxed{D}, no extra text.

Final version:

New-onset proteinuria, thrombocytopenia <100,000/mL, or severe hypertension >160/110 mmHg indicate superimposed preeclampsia in women with chronic hypertension. Retinal hyperemia reflects chronic hypervolemia and vessel remodeling without signifying acute disease flare-up.  

$\boxed{D}$
